title: Line Motions
subtitle: 0 / ^ / $ — the three places that matter on a line.
summary: 0 jumps to column 0. ^ jumps to the first non-blank character. $ jumps to the end of the line. They're motions, so d$ deletes to end of line, c0 changes to start.

blocks.text: A line has three interesting positions: the actual start (column 0), the first non-blank character (where the code begins), and the end. Vim gives them three keys.
{key:^} is what you almost always want. {key:0} is for when the leading whitespace itself is the target — for example, {key:d}{key:0} to delete the indentation.

blocks.title: Inclusive vs Exclusive — Why $ Behaves Subtly
blocks.text:
Motions in Vim have a *type* that decides what an operator does with them:

  • **Exclusive** motions don't include the character under the destination cursor. {key:w} is exclusive: {key:d}{key:w} deletes up to but not including the next word's first character.
  • **Inclusive** motions do include it. {key:e} is inclusive: {key:d}{key:e} deletes through the end of the word.
  • **Linewise** motions act on whole lines. {key:j} is linewise: {key:d}{key:j} deletes two whole lines.

{key:0} is exclusive; {key:^} is exclusive; {key:$} is **inclusive** — that's why {key:d}{key:$} deletes through end-of-line *including* the last character. The Universal Grammar (operator + motion) keeps working, but the precise byte ranges depend on the motion's type. Full classification: [`appendix.motion-classification`](#appendix.motion-classification).

There's a shortcut: {key:D} = {key:d}{key:$} (delete to end of line). {key:C} = {key:c}{key:$}. {key:Y} *should* be {key:y}{key:$} but historically isn't — see the wart in [`editing.yank-put`](#editing.yank-put).
